title: Total anatomical reconstruction during robot-assisted radical prostatectomy: Update of continence recovery data after more than 600 procedures
Total anatomical reconstruction during robot-assisted radical prostatectomy: Continence recovery in more than 800 procedures after one year of follow-up

abstract:
<jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      To present the functional and oncological outcomes after ≥1 year of follow‐up, following an experience of >1000 robot‐assisted radical prostatectomies (
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">RARPs</jats:styled-content>
                      ) with our standardised total anatomical reconstruction (
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">TAR</jats:styled-content>
                      ) technique. To evaluate which factors influence postoperative continence recovery in order to obtain a nomogram to predict the risk of postoperative urinary incontinence (UI).
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Patients and Methods</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      The enrolment phase began in June 2013 and ended in May 2017. Patients were prospectively included in the study with the following inclusion criteria: (i) localised prostate cancer (clinical stages
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">cT</jats:styled-content>
                      1–3,
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">cN</jats:styled-content>
                      0,
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">cM</jats:styled-content>
                      0); (ii) indication for RP; and (iii) preoperative multiparametric prostate magnetic resonance imaging. All patients underwent
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">RARP</jats:styled-content>
                      with the
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">TAR</jats:styled-content>
                      technique done at the end of the demolitive phase. The continence rates were assessed at 24 h, and 1, 4, 12, 24 and 48 weeks after catheter removal. Patients were defined as continent if they answered ‘zero pad’ or ‘one safety pad’ per day. A logistic regression model was used to evaluate the potential impact of some pre‐ and intraoperative factors on postoperative urinary continence recovery. Model discrimination was assessed using an area under (
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">AUC</jats:styled-content>
                      ) the receiver operating characteristic (
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">ROC</jats:styled-content>
                      ) curve. A nomogram to predict the risk of postoperative UI after
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">RARP</jats:styled-content>
                      with
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">the TAR</jats:styled-content>
                      technique was generated based on the logistic model.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      In all, 1008 patients were enrolled in our study. At 24 h, and 1, 4, 12, 24 and 48 weeks after catheter removal, 621 (61.61%), 594 (58.93%), 803 (79.66%), 912 (90.48%), 950 (94.25%) and 956 (94.84%) patients were continent, respectively. In the logistic regression model, the variables analysed had a higher impact on continence recovery at 4 and 12 weeks. At 4 weeks, the postoperative odds of urinary continence recovery increased with the absence of diabetes [odds ratio (
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">OR)</jats:styled-content>
                      2.76, 95%
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">confidence interval (CI)</jats:styled-content>
                      1.41–5.41] and D'Amico low vs high risk (
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">OR</jats:styled-content>
                      2.01, 95%
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">CI</jats:styled-content>
                      1.01–3.99). At 12 weeks, urinary continence increased with the absence of diabetes (
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">OR</jats:styled-content>
                      3.01, 95%
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">CI</jats:styled-content>
                      1.23–7.35), D'Amico low vs high risk (
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">OR</jats:styled-content>
                      4.04, 95%
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">CI</jats:styled-content>
                       1.56–10.47), and D'Amico intermediate vs high risk (
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">OR</jats:styled-content>
                       3.33, 95%
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">CI</jats:styled-content>
                      1.66–6.70).
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">ROC</jats:styled-content>
                      curves were drawn and an
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">AUC</jats:styled-content>
                      value of 61.9% (95%
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">CI</jats:styled-content>
                      57.49–66.36) at 4 weeks and 63.8% (95%
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">CI</jats:styled-content>
                      58.03–69.65) at 12 weeks were computed. Based on these parameters, two nomograms (at 4 and 12 weeks postoperatively) were generated.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      The
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">TAR</jats:styled-content>
                      technique conferred excellent results in the early recovery of urinary continence. Two nomograms were created, to predict preoperatively the postoperative odds of urinary continence recovery at 4 and 12 weeks after
                      <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">RARP</jats:styled-content>
                      by integrating the presence of diabetes and D'Amico risk classification.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
title: Total anatomical reconstruction during robot‐assisted radical prostatectomy: focus on urinary continence recovery and related complications after 1000 procedures
